ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301583 covers oyster as a food item; no Canada-specific raw oyster term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 covers oyster as a substance; no Canada-specific or raw-serving variant exists. Closest parent used.

main_ingredients: fresh oysters (Crassostrea gigas or Ostrea edulis, Canadian origin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of fresh Canadian oysters is approximately 2 pieces (roughly 100 g edible flesh), typically served raw on the half shell with lemon or mignonette sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, eastern, wild, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Canadian oysters (primarily Pacific Crassostrea gigas or Atlantic Crassostrea virginica) are nutritionally comparable to USDA SR Legacy eastern wild raw oysters. Values adapted from USDA FDC ID 175159 as the closest approved-source match for raw oyster flesh per 100 g. No Canada-specific raw oyster entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved secondary source for this raw seafood ingredient.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Canadian oysters specifically. Raw oysters and shellfish are very low-carbohydrate foods (3.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly glycogen). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) classify shellfish and seafood with negligible starch as GI ≤ 5. A GI of 5 is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate for raw oysters, consistent with the low-GI classification of other bivalve molluscs. GL per 100 g serving = (5 × 3.9) / 100 = 0.2.

hypertension.guidance: Fresh raw oysters contain approximately 417 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce, while benefiting from the potassium (168 mg) and omega-3 fatty acids that support vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ≈ 5) and low carbohydrate content (3.9 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fresh Canadian oysters have a very low glycemic index of approximately 5 and a glycemic load of only 0.2 per 100 g serving, making them an excellent protein-rich, low-GI food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; their high zinc content (39.3 mg per 100 g) may also support insulin metabolism.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, low saturated fat, and high selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh Canadian oysters provide omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (0.9 g per 100 g), low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), and high selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), all of which are cardioprotective; however, their moderate sodium content (417 mg per 100 g) warrants mindful consumption in individuals with established cardiovascular disease or hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Canadian Oysters are raw, live bivalve molluscs harvested from Canadian coastal waters, primarily from British Columbia (Pacific oysters, Crassostrea gigas) and the Atlantic provinces (eastern oysters, Crassostrea virginica). They are served minimally processed — typically shucked and presented on the half shell — and are prized for their clean, briny, oceanic flavour with a smooth, slightly firm yet tender texture. In Singapore's seafood restaurant scene, fresh Canadian oysters are a premium offering, often served with lemon wedges, Tabasco, or mignonette sauce. Nutritionally, raw oysters are exceptionally rich in zinc (approximately 39 mg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (8.75 mcg per 100 g), selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), and iron (5.1 mg per 100 g), making them one of the most micronutrient-dense foods available. They provide moderate protein (7.1 g per 100 g) and are low in calories (68 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (3.9 g per 100 g), with a very low glycemic index of approximately 5. Clinically, they are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to their negligible glycemic impact, and their omega-3 fatty acid content supports cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate at 417 mg per 100 g, which should be considered by individuals with hypertension. Oysters are a shellfish allergen and are not halal-certified in most contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Canadian oysters?
What nutrients are in raw oysters?
Is oyster good for heart health?
How much zinc is in oysters?
Are Canadian oysters safe to eat raw?
What is the glycemic index of oysters?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159) for eastern wild raw oysters, which is the closest approved-source match for raw oyster flesh. Canadian oysters (Pacific or Atlantic) are nutritionally comparable but not identical to USDA eastern oysters; minor variation in zinc, selenium, and omega-3 content may exist depending on harvest region and season. No direct HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for Canadian oysters. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (eastern wild raw oysters) as the closest approved-source match; Canadian Pacific and Atlantic oysters are nutritionally comparable. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC source; oysters do not contain industrially produced trans fats. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC source; oyster carbohydrate is predominantly glycogen, not free sugars. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FDC source; oysters are animal-origin foods with no dietary fibre. 5. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classification of shellfish and bivalves as very low GI; no direct Canadian oyster GI measurement exists in the approved GI database. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.4), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), potassium_mg (168.0), calcium_mg (45.0), iron_mg (5.1), cholesterol_mg (53.0), vitamin_c_mg (3.7), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (16.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.07), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.15), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.5), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), vitamin_b12_mcg (8.75), folate_mcg_dfe (10.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.8), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (135.0), magnesium_mg (22.0), zinc_mg (39.3), selenium_mcg (63.7), water_g (85.1) — all reported from USDA FDC ID 175159. 7. Halal status is marked false as oysters are not universally accepted as halal under all Islamic jurisprudence schools (Hanafi school considers bivalves impermissible). 8. The input food_name contained an emoji ('👍') which has been removed to produce a canonical English food name. 9. Cuisine tag in input was 'Chinese' but the food origin is Canadian; cuisine field updated to 'Seafood' to reflect the food's actual identity as a Canadian seafood product served in a Singapore seafood restaurant context.
